text: Neurologist Ann Graybiel's MIT lab demonstrated in the 1990s that rats navigating a maze initially showed diffuse neural activity throughout the brain. As the route became habitual, activity collapsed into a brief spike at the maze's start and another at its end — the basal ganglia had compressed the entire sequence. Graybiel called this process 'chunking': the brain bundles a chain of actions into one automated unit to conserve cognitive energy. This efficiency is a feature, not a flaw. But it also explains why habitual behaviors feel effortless and invisible — they run below the threshold of conscious awareness, making them remarkably resistant to deliberate change.
question: What did Graybiel's rat maze experiments reveal about neural activity as behavior becomes habitual?

options.text: Activity spread across more brain regions as the route was memorized
The prefrontal cortex took over primary control of the sequence
Activity compressed into brief spikes at the start and end of the sequence
The hippocampus replaced the basal ganglia as the dominant structure
